text: Multicomponent reactions (MCRs) stand as one of the cornerstones of sustainable chemistry due to their high atom economy and high procedural efficiency toward the synthesis of complex molecules in a single step. Although MCRs have been developed over many decades and are widely used in diversity-oriented synthesis, the detailed mechanisms of many of the basic MCRs remain a topic of discussion in the scientific community. This review aims to summarize the current understanding of mechanisms for five of the most basic MCRs, namely Biginelli, Hantzsch, Mannich, Passerini, and Ugi reactions. Based on experimental and theoretical evidence, the occurrence of different mechanisms for these reactions is reviewed and discussed, taking into account the roles of iminium and enamine species as well as Knoevenagel products. The experimental data, including NMR and MS, as well as the predictions of density functional theory (DFT) calculations are integrated and used to discuss the influence of reaction conditions, catalysts, and solvents on the mechanism of each reaction. Finally, some of the hot points and side reactions are discussed in order to give some possible strategies for the development of new catalysts with higher efficiency, selectivity, and sustainability for the production of valuable chemicals and pharmaceuticals.
